facts_disclosed: CIMA plan dropped — ACA alone is hard enough, no bandwidth

turns.content: so remember how some people talk about doing multiple professional qualifications at the same time like it's no big deal? yeah those people are either lying or they don't have a life
Ha — I take it you've been experiencing the reality of juggling qualifications alongside actual work? What's going on?

okay so basically a while ago I had this idea that I was going to do CIMA on top of my ACA. like double qualified, looks amazing on the CV, PwC might sponsor it, the whole thing. I was properly gassed about it

That's a pretty ambitious plan — ACA and CIMA together would definitely make you stand out. Both are respected qualifications and having both covers management accounting and chartered accounting. What made you interested in adding CIMA on top?

I thought it would open up more doors long term. like ACA is more audit and financial reporting focused, and CIMA gives you the management accounting and business strategy side. I figured if I did both I'd be properly versatile. but honestly? yeah the whole CIMA idea is done
Dead as in you've decided not to pursue it? What changed your thinking?

yeah dead dead. like not "maybe later" dead. actually properly off the table. I'm already struggling to juggle ACA with the job as it is. the idea of adding another qualification on top of that would actually finish me off. I don't know what I was thinking tbh

That sounds like a really pragmatic call, honestly. ACA on its own is a serious commitment — what, like 15 exams across three levels while holding down a demanding full-time role? That's already a huge undertaking. Trying to layer CIMA on top of that wouldn't just be hard, it would probably compromise both.

exactly!! like the ACA exams alone are doing my head in. I'm working these long days and then coming home and having to revise and my brain is just mush by that point. adding CIMA modules on top? I'd burn out in about 3 weeks wallah

Burnout is a real risk, especially in a Big 4 environment where the hours are already intense. There's nothing strategic about spreading yourself so thin that you underperform in everything. Better to do ACA well and actually absorb the material than to rush through two qualifications half-prepared.

Do you feel okay about the decision, or is there part of you that's still a bit disappointed?

I mean there's a tiny part of me that's like ugh because double qualified sounds so sick on a CV. but realistically? pretend I never said anything loool. it was one of those ideas that sounds amazing at 2am when you're scrolling career forums and then in the cold light of day you're like babes... no

The 2am career forum pipeline is dangerously persuasive — everything sounds achievable when you're in that headspace. But the cold light of day version of you is making the better call.

And honestly, being ACA qualified from a Big 4 firm is already a very strong position. It's one of the most recognised accounting qualifications in the world. You don't need to stack another one on top to be competitive — you need to actually pass the ACA exams without running yourself into the ground.

that's what my manager said too actually. she was like Aisha the ACA is more than enough, focus on doing it properly and getting good scores rather than trying to collect qualifications like pokemon cards loool

Your manager sounds smart — and the fact that she's telling you ACA alone is sufficient should carry real weight. She's seen the careers play out, she knows what actually matters for progression, and she's essentially telling you that quality beats quantity.

The Pokemon cards analogy is also very accurate. Qualifications have diminishing returns — the first one transforms your career, the second one is nice to have, the third one just makes people wonder when you actually do any work.

looool okay yeah fair point. no honestly I feel good about it. it's one less thing on my plate and I actually feel lighter now that I've properly accepted it's not happening. I was holding onto it as like a "maybe" for months and that was stressing me out too because it felt like something I should be doing

That "should" pressure is the worst — carrying around a plan you know you can't realistically execute but feeling guilty for not doing it. Officially killing it is actually a productive decision because it frees up mental energy. You're not just removing the workload of CIMA, you're removing the guilt of not doing CIMA.

Now you can put all that energy into your ACA prep and your actual job without that background noise. How's the ACA going at the moment — are you in the middle of a study block?

yeah I've got my next sitting coming up in a couple months so I'm trying to be consistent with revision rather than doing the last minute cramming thing I did last time. it's going okay tbh. like I understand the material I just need to practice more exam technique

Exam technique is honestly half the battle with ACA — understanding the content is one thing, but knowing how to structure your answers under time pressure is what separates good scores from scraping by.

If you want to sharpen that:

- Do timed past papers under proper exam conditions — no notes, no phone, strict time limit
- After each one, compare your answers to the model answers and note where you're losing marks (usually it's not knowledge gaps, it's answer structure or time allocation)
- Focus on the first and last five minutes — most marks are won in how you plan your answer and how you wrap it up

The fact that you're starting consistent revision early instead of cramming is already a massive improvement. Sounds like you're in a good spot.

yeah I think so. and honestly just having one qualification to focus on instead of two makes the whole thing feel way more manageable. thanks for not making me feel bad about dropping it. some people would be like "oh but you should push yourself" and it's like bro I AM pushing myself, just not in a direction that leads to a breakdown loool

Knowing what to say no to is genuinely one of the most underrated professional skills. You're not giving up on ambition — you're being smart about where you direct it. There's a massive difference between the two.

Focus on ACA, do it well, get qualified, and then reassess what you want after that. You can always explore CIMA or something else later down the line if it makes sense. But right now, one thing done properly beats two things done badly.

Good luck with the next sitting — you've got this.
